steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage contrasts traditional sonnet cycles (Spenser's Amoretti, which follows a romantic-pursuit pattern) with McKay's "Cities," which uses the same form for an autobiographical travel narrative. The question asks what conclusion the passage most strongly supports.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Although sonnet cycles are strongly associated with a certain subject matter, this association doesn't preclude poets from exploring ideas that are relatively unorthodox by the genre's standards.: matches the passage exactly. Spenser establishes the romantic tradition; McKay "subverts entrenched sonnet conventions" by using the form for travel narrative. ✓
  • Choice B — Although sonnet cycles generally feature similar narrative arcs related to the speaker's relationship with another person, there is a remarkable range in the number of perspectives represented within the story arcs of different sonnet cycles.: the passage never discusses varying "perspectives" on sonnet cycles — it discusses subject matter, not point of view. ✗
  • Choice C — Although sonnet cycles generally present an overarching narrative structure, some of those narratives, such as the narrative in McKay's "Cities," are more cohesive than others.: the passage does not evaluate whether sonnet cycles are cohesive or not. ✗
  • Choice D — Although the rigidity of the conventions of sonnet cycles discourages experimentation, sonnet cycles sometimes exhibit truly novel features, as exemplified by McKay's "Cities" and Spenser's Amoretti.: the passage presents Amoretti as the established tradition, not as something innovative. ✗
That is the correct answer.
